title: 2025/11/20 - ENV (Part 2 of 2)
displayTitle: 2025-11-20 - Environment Committee - Fast-track Approvals Amendment Bill [Part 2]
description: Thursday, 20th November 2025 | Environment Committee Fast-track Approvals Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence: Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment Simon Upton, Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment Rebecca Thornley, General Council Panel Convenor for the Fast Track Approvals Act 2024 Jane Borthwick, Panel Convenor Jennifer Caldwell, Associate Convenor
descriptionSummary: Fast-track Approvals Amendment Bill. Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment. Simon Upton, Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment

chunks.text: Recording is paused. Rachel, you're, you're, you're safe. I was Wondering if he was hiding around the corner making

Public session and back to our submissions on the Fast Track approvals Amendment Bill. And we welcome the Parliamentary Commissioner for the environment, Simon, to the committee this morning. Uh, this afternoon, actually this afternoon. Uh, and you've got 20 minutes with us. Uh, take your submission as read, uh, and we'll open up to you for your opening remarks.

Well, thank you Madam Chair, and, and I, I do hope you have had time to read it, and I appreciate that you haven't had a lot of time. Yes, it is, um, better crafted than what I'm going to say to you, but it would take much longer if I tried to read it. And I want to leave you time for questions. Obviously. Look, I've got nothing to offer the committee on the provisions that relate to supermarkets or some of the minor technical amendments that have received broad support.

My comments are confined to the radical proposals that seek to re-engineer the fast track process in midstream. As you'll be aware, I hope from my written submission, I consider that the existing fast track process is working well, better frankly, than I expected.

That's due to two things principally. Firstly, the wise decision to leave critical judgements and determinations to independent properly qualified panels, empowered to inform themselves about the key dimensions of what's proposed. And secondly, the very professional way in which the EPA and the panel conveners have gone about establishing a process that is both, uh, transparent and swift and gives individual cases the best chance of being properly dealt with.

This bill torpedoes these two positive elements below the waterline in the short time available. I won't dwell on the practical process problems the bill raises, although my submission refers to them, they're expertly canvassed by the panel conveners who I think are following me. These are people of real substance and experience, and if they tell you something as amiss, I suggest you listen because restricting timeframes could actually backfire and undermine the whole process.

I want instead to use the very limited time available to me to focus on the two most egregious, substantive provisions, and they, in my view, are clause five, dealing with government policy statements and Clause 33, dealing with input by affected persons. Now, the proposal in clause five that ministers should be able to concoct bespoke government policy statements, which panels must consider, is a thinly veiled attempt to guarantee a favorable decision.

The government is asking parliament, government's asking you Parliament to give it an open-ended power to determine what national regional benefit will mean for any particular type of infrastructure or development. And in the absence of any transitional provisions, these policy statements can be applied to applications that are already in train.

They've already got panels hearing them. This is executive overreach for which no case has been made. It is as open-ended as the infamous Economic Stabilization Act that allowed governments to regulate absolutely anything if they thought it threatened economic stability.

I've really taken aback that the government should be asking parliament to license ministers to issue what the New Zealand initiative charitably, I think describes as opaque ministerial directions. There are no guardrails if you enact this provision, no guardrails.

Ministers will be able to design bespoke policy statements for particular types of projects in an attempt to provide certainty for specific applications. The vague drafting of Clause five means that ministers could, for example, instruct panelists that as a matter of government policy mining is of significant regional benefit when proposed in the Corra Mandel or on Great Barrier Island.

And that in weighing the benefits of any project, quantifiable economic benefits must be given greater weight than less easily quantified intangible environmental cost or risk, which will pass to future generations. There is nothing to prevent something as directive as that.

That's the key point about the legislation that's been put in front of you. The amendments appear intended to whittle away the autonomy of panel conveners and expert panels by making the decision making boundaries as narrow as possible in an attempt to orbit guarantee approval for projects that ministers deemed to be good for us.

Which begs the question, why have a panel at all if you can even find members to appoint? And I think it will be hard to get panel members to serve if you pass this into law. And also it creates a very strong incentive to lobby ministers for special favors with all the risks that entails mm-hmm.

Definitions which contribute to the decision making criteria that a panel must consider should be prescribed by you, by parliament. And some submitters have offered views on how this could be achieved if the committee thinks that definition is necessary. While I'm on the topic of ministerial powers, you'll note in my submission a discussion of new powers to direct the EPA on the performance of its functions and duties and the exercise of its powers.

I encourage the committee to query this with officials and in particular, satisfy yourself that Clause 48 can't be used as a mechanism for directing panels and conveners. Now let me turn to clause 33, which replaces the ability of the panel to invite comments from any other person the panel considers appropriate with a limitation that the panel can only invite comments from other persons if the relevant local authorities or agencies do not intend to comment on the matter or if the comments they intend to make will not sufficiently address the

matter how I won. Are panels supposed to know whether local authorities are going to sufficiently address a matter? More importantly, since when have local authorities become the only legitimate mouthpieces for public concern as a rate payer, I don't think they're, it will certainly place enormous pressure on local authorities as the only voice for community and wider public concerns.

Now I have, uh, with me here today, team members Shannon Watson, who I recruited specifically because of his practical experience and consenting matters. He's actually been involved in some fast track stuff. If you've got questions about what impact the additional pressures imposed by this bill might have on overstretched local authorities, he may be able to help you.

The bill seeks to significantly reduce the ability of people whose property and lives could be placed at risk to even express their views to panels. Some of you who are on the committee that heard submissions on the original bill, which is now law, may recall that I mentioned the air case.

You may recall that the air case and the oral submission I made to you air was an application for a very large above ground water storage dam in North Canterbury. Uh, issues in the early stage of the hearing were of a highly technical engineering nature, but they suddenly became real because during a a a break in the hearings, the kaikoura earthquake happened.

Now it emerged that in the event of a dam breach, about 200 homes were located in what would be the flow path of fast moving flood waters stretching for some kilometers below the site and located along small unsealed rural roads. Now, I expressed concern about those people, uh, having the ability to, uh, actually put something to a panel before.

But these are the sort of people a panel would under this bill no longer be able to seek comment from panel, could not seek comment from those people. Now, I think committee members should ask themselves if they would want to live below a tailings dam or a reservoir which could fail, but have no ability to contribute their views to a process in which the government has decreed that there are benefits.

And where a decision is being made under punishing time constraints. It seems to me that something like a tailings dam will inevitably impact on property values downstream of that structure. If that's the case, then the decision to fast track a mining consent favors one person's property rights at the expense of another without giving the latter any chance to have their say.

Finally, there has been no serious regulatory analysis of this bill, and I strongly suggest that the committee seeks detailed information from officials in the Ministry for Regulation or the Minister to explain the absence of a policy rationale or regulatory impact analysis.

That's enough from me. I think we have a little time for questions. Thank You. Uh, thank you commissioner for that very expressive, passionate contribution there. I will go first to Rachel Brooking, who has a question. And then Celia, Thank you. And thank you for focusing on those two things, uh, being clause five and clause 33, and I want to discuss both of them with you and, uh, question whether clause five with, uh, this reference to government or the government policy statements that you're talking about that I think

you were quoting from some, uh, the New Zealand Institute saying that they were an opaque ministerial decision Direction. Direction. So, um, if you've got comments on if there's a way to fix that, I'm interested in terms of definitions of what a, what they, what the criteria could be for them and what the constraints would be.

Um, I'm also though more interested in the focus on things, uh, getting into the fast track process and that what this is what seems the, um, government policy statements are about and an opportunity to come into the process. And you're shaking your head. So I'm interested in the, the consequences of being able to come into a process when we already had 149 projects come into the process via the schedule and the committee stage of the fast track approvals bill.

And some of those projects are very controversial, have been turned down before. How can this be any worse? Is my first question around that. That's my framing. But how Can this, what is this, this Work, oh, this change to enable more things through, through a nebulous government policy statement.

We dunno what it could be about. How can that, how can that be worse? One question, and then is it limited to the access to the process rather than the decision making, which is your second point? And your second point seems to be con um, about the constraints that decision makers will have and how that will lead to worse decisions.

Well look on, on, uh, I hand to my general counsel for the legal point to reply to a lawyer, but, uh, the reason I was shaking my head is it's not limited to entry into the process. It, it is comp, there are no guardrails. You could write a bespoke policy for anything and that, so as legislators, I'm saying to you, do you really want a provision which gives ministers the power to write anything they like?

Now I'll, I'll, I'll let, I'll let my colleague here reply in a more focused legal way. Yeah, I'll deal with the criteria that you could put around GPS, um, other submitters have commented on. But just to, to follow on from what you've been saying, Simon, with regards to entry to the process versus decision making considerations, the g the government policy statements would have an impact on entry to the process, to the effect that, uh, the minister's decision to refer would need to consider the minister's statements

that they've issued under that clause. So it would potentially, uh, be more permissive in that respect. Uh, but I would just temper that in terms of answering the member's question just to reorientate that, um, the listed projects, whilst they have particular access, uh, to the fast track process, it doesn't fast track their substantive application.

No. So they must still make a substantive application. So it doesn't mean they're in the process, they've just leapfrogged over the referral process. Um, in relation to how you could put criteria around government policy statements to give them some more rigor, uh, Simon has mentioned in his submission, um, uh, the, on what basis those are being made.

So whether or not you'd want to include criteria around how you generate a government policy statement, for example, will it be informed by a cost benefit analysis or is it simply a pluck? Um, you'll see that the RMLA has provided a table of, uh, potential solutions to some of the issues that have been raised by many submitters, um, appreciating that that has all been done at extreme haste.

So they're not, uh, perfect solutions, but they certainly provide some, uh, suggestions. If you look at the national policy statements under the RMA as a potential template, uh, to draw criteria from, so a bit more transparency around what is proposed, would you consult on them? How long would that consultation period be?

Um, would you put any parameters around what could or could not be? For example, may a government policy statement names specific applications. So there's a real lack of prescription around what types of infrastructure and development is being referred to in clause five. But, but just to cap that off, can I say, I don't think you need these government policy statements, period.

Uh, I wouldn't be spending too much time trying to craft, uh, careful criteria. There's no evidence that they are needed. You have a system that is working good, people are asking the right questions in quite constrained timeframes. Uh, and I think it could be a lot worse. Uh, is the short answer to your question, just Can I ask a s on that?

Yeah. Just because you did talk about putting some criteria around the government policy statements. Would that in your view, uh, commissioner provide the guardrails that you are talking about? It might, depends what's in them, but currently there's absolutely nothing. And as I've said to you, I don't believe you need these government policy statements.

I mean, this is just, we're back down the track of the expansion of the ministerial and executive power. You know, you sign off a law and say, we'll leave that to the minister, and they can make it up as they like, I mean, you, you, of course you can constrain anything, but we haven't been given any analysis which says there's a problem Panels needed, government policy statements.

I don't think they did. You mean ask ask the panel convenience what they think about that. But I don't think that that's, that the case for doing anything has not been made And they're coming in next. So is your question on clause five and that same, because, um, Pam, that's why question's been asked by Okay. It has. Okay, there we go. Carry on. So The, the well, and it was related to then the constraints of the decision makers once, once the decision makers get it.

Um, and just to check that you are saying, um, that this, this means that things can get a lot worse when the, when the panel conveners can't ask the people down the road in your ear example, that will make decision making worse. Yeah, absolutely. As I say, how, how are you supposed to know? If you say, oh, no, no, the school will be handled by the local authority.

How are you supposed to know the local authority will handle a matter satisfactorily? Do you wanna add some? Sure. I think I would refrain from describing it as making decision making worse. The expert panels do the best they can with the information that's before them. What it may risk is robust decision making as the outcome from the fast track process.

Mm-hmm. So when we talk about robust, it's evidence based, it's information based, and ultimately from a legal perspective, it's defensible. Yeah. And if you haven't got robust decision making as an outcome of this process, then judicial review will be the only avenue. Mm-hmm. And we'll then to use Simon's words, torpedo the process in an entirely different way.

Thank you. Look, we'll go to Celia now. Thank you. Um, I've got some questions from your submission and I do admire the, um, language you use. You're not being mincing words there. Um, somebody described this bill as a Trojan supermarket and that it is disguised with a, something we're all keen on, which is reducing grocery prices, but it actually goes far wider and invades as the Trojan horse did, invades the fast track bill with other issues.

Well, look, governments have legislative vehicles. They want to poke things into them. I've got no objection. If they want to do something on supermarkets, I've got no objection. If they wanna do something of a technical nature, I do have an objection. If they're putting very, very significant substantive changes into a bill without making the case.

I mean, you do not have policy analysis or regulatory impact analysis to support it. Um, so I think that's how I would respond. And handing following on from that, with the ministerial role of the government policy statement, as I understand it, and we've been a minister and I have not, so you might be able to enlighten me So long ago, what it's like

A minister can take needs to take their policy statement to other relevant portfolios, but they don't need to get the agreement from the other portfolio. So it might be something that was going to be hugely damaging to, let's say conservation take it to the Minister of Conservation and just having taken it to them rather than getting their agreement would be sufficient.

Is that your understanding? Well, I can, I can get my lawyer to answer that, but look, the practical reality is ministers will operate their affairs behind their closed doors. They'll reach agreements. I mean, if, you know that that's, that's the reality. I don't think there's much to be to found there, but, but they're not, But they're not, sorry, I was trying to, to be clearer.

The minister does not have to get agreement from the other portfolio. They just have to tell 'em about it. Yeah, but I mean, how would you ever know? I, I'll, I'll let a lawyer answer this in a more scholarly way. I practical terms, I can tell you if a minister, uh, has a policy they've consulted and then they go ahead with it one way or another, the government's agreed that that's okay.

But would you like to comment on the legal drafting? Look, I think you'll find across the statute books, it's littered with references where a minister must consult with other relevant portfolio ministers. It's in the Fast Track act at the moment. Yeah. Um, and I think that's all you can reasonably expect in terms of drafting for this given cabinet collective responsibility for that are taken in the first place.

Exactly. But what's really missing, and to use conservation as an example, is anybody who enjoys the conservation values of that land are being excluded from the process. So a government policy statement is being generated without any transparent inputs. Consultation Yeah. Or decisions are Being made. That's the more significant point. Yeah.

So no specialist knowledge. No, thank you. Look, we've got other members who want to ask questions and we are running out of time, so we'll go to ground for column now. Yeah. Some question, it's interesting your comment that obviously when the first Fast Track bill came through, you made the comment that, you know, you, you had doubts about it big time, and you submitted, you know, your concerns, yet you're now, you're now saying that currently it's working well, you, it's working pretty well, you're reasonably happy

with the way it is working. Um, what, why couldn't that then become the case with Cliff's version? Because, Because this version has no guardrails, it's got no protections. Stuff can just come from heaven, bing, and apply even to stuff which is already in the process.

I mean, I think the, the, my view of Fast Track as it now exists, it's not perfect and it's putting a lot of pressure on people and it's early days. We can't yet say it's unmitigated success. But for the reasons I gave, the people who have the EPA and the panel conveners have sat down and said, okay, within these timeframes, how can we make this work?

It is, it is pretty transparent. And panels are demonstrating that they are actually interested in taking information from people other than the narrow list. Yeah. So to speak. Uh, what this, what this is trying to do is to take away all of the sort of buffer and flexibility that is in the existing legislation.

I don't think you can fix this. The best thing is to drop this and have some confidence in what you actually enacted. Thank you very much. We will have to leave it there. We have met time, and thank you for your insights and, and thorough. Sorry, I couldn't answer submission question. Thank you. Anyone's welcome to come and talk to us.

By the way, if that we're just across the road, if you want to go into some detail, I've got people who can help You. You know, we appreciate that. Thank very much. Thank you. We here though at the moment, So Yes. Urgency. So, uh, hence why we're moving through pretty quickly. So we, uh, have next up the panel convener for the fast Track approvals at 2024.

Welcome, welcome to the select committee this afternoon. You have 20 minutes with us also. So, uh, we'll hand over to you the opening remarks, but take the submission as read and members of course will have questions. So, over to you. All right, thank you very much. Uh, my name is Jane Wick and I am the panel convener for the Fast Track Approvals Act.

And I'm here today presenting with Jennifer Caldwell an associate panel convener. Uh, we will not be addressing the policy and the amendment bill, but we wish to acknowledge, acknowledge the gravity of the issues that this committee will be working through. And I urge the committee to be alert for unintended policy outcomes and to proceed with caution.

Here. I am pleased to take the, uh, Madam Chair's, uh, guidance, uh, that the panel convener submission has been read. The submission deals with the practicality of six amendments. We're happy to take questions in relation to those amendments, but we're also happy to take questions in relation to the implementation of the act.

The key message is this, it is early days, but the act as currently framed is working well. It's working as was intended. The purpose of the Fast Track Approvals Act is to facilitate the delivery of infrastructure and development projects that provide significant regional and national benefits.

The legislation responds to broad cross party recognition of New Zealand's infrastructure deficit and the need to support sustainable economic growth. And the key features of your existing act are, which facilitates infrastructure and development, are that the act is a one-stop shop for approvals.

Applications are not notified the timeframe to release a decision set on a case by case basis. By the panel convener. Panels of experts are being appointed for their knowledge, skills, and expertise. There is no right of appeal to the environment court and appeals to the high court are on matters of law.

Only the acts inquiry led approach was new to New Zealand law, and this has created the opportunity for a credible alternative environmental practice to emerge and to address some of the underlying drivers of environmental, uh, law reform. The work of the panel convenience has been on process redesign on cutting through the outdated, unhelpful modes of practice.

And this is essential to deliver on this acts purpose and this ACT's promise. So the markets of success for this le legislation are as follows. First, the extensive positive engagement between key stakeholders, including local authorities, ministering agencies, practitioners, and the legal profession.

With the panel conveners, New Zealand is working hard to deliver this act. Second, the caliber of experts attracted and appointed to the panels and the robustness of their decisions. This legislation is complex and it needs to be, but there are risks created by advancing impractical amendments that the panel and those risks are addressed in the panel co convenance submissions.

But the risks arise in seeking, uh, seeking speed without corresponding change in environmental practice, while at the same time leaving untouched the acts robust decision making directions in practical unworkable changes will simply concentrate pressure on panels without delivering the much needed reform of environmental practice.

And where this to happen, the quality of some decisions will be very vulnerable to judicial review and appeal. I'm gonna hand over now to my colleague Jennifer. Thanks. Thank you. Committee. I just wanna start with a couple of statistics that you might be interested in. First is that we've appointed 23 panels to date since the portal opened with the EPA in February.

That process has delivered five final decisions to approve projects. There's two draft decisions currently out for comment at the moment. There'll be another three decisions provided by the end of the year, potentially a fourth because one or two panels are running ahead of schedule. So you can expect a good 10 approval decisions, uh, on important projects, but to be delivered by the end of the year, that's in a period of less than 10 months, depending on when those applications were lodged.

Now, um, our view is that that is really a very promising start to the implementation of this process. Um, there were 149 projects listed in the second schedule. We can only set up panels at a rate at which applications are lodged. We know that there are a number of applications that will not be made because proponents have decided not to advance them at this time or perhaps have decided to advance them under the more traditional process for various reasons.

Uh, but literally it's not as if there are 120 projects waiting for us to set up panels. Uh, we set them up as in when the applications are lodged, checked is complete and then provided to us. Um, there are a couple of panels due to be set up next week. Three, in fact, I think will be appointed next week.

Uh, so the process is working and it's a little bit, I have to say it was a little bit surprising to us to see the tender of some of these amendments, uh, which appear to be driven by, uh, some commentary, uh, to the government that somehow the process is not working as well as it should or not working as fast as it should or could.

Uh, our view is it's working well, uh, and that is despite some quite challenging, uh, timeframes involved. Uh, I should also say that as well as speaking for ourselves and being available to you to answer questions about the actual process of setting up panels. Uh, to some extent we are here on behalf of panels because they don't have a voice in this process.

We're talking about, you know, a couple of hundred people who have, you know, um, been willing to take appointment to one of these panels, um, and to devote the time and the discipline needed to work through this process to deliver robust decisions. Uh, they're not in front of you, but we are also very mindful of the pressures that are being brought to bear on them already and which may intensify if some of these amendments go through.

Uh, I know they've been described as relatively minor technical or mechanical changes, um, to speed up processing time, for example. But we do think, as Jane has said, that there is a danger here that there could be unforeseen or unintended consequences. Our view is that the act works as a cohesive hole.

You might think of it like a spider web. You pull on one strand that sets off vibrations or ripples throughout the whole structure. So what we've tried to focus our submission on is some of those ripple effects that may not have been anticipated. And those are very much in the realm of workability of the process.

Um, I was responsible for setting up the panels to deal with the Yhe North application for the Taranaki VTM application and also for the Port of Toga Stella Passage application. If you have questions about the process or the challenges that were involved in setting up those panels and fixing those timeframes, I'm very happy to answer those questions.

I would say, however, that those are three of the most contentious projects or complex projects that were in the second schedule, that they were lodged very early on in the, um, in the, in the implementation of the act. And that they had a range of quite unique challenges because all of them had been through some form of earlier consenting process that either, uh, was unsuccessful or did not reach a conclusion which left a number of unanswered issues and questions and a number of parties whose engagement was cut short.

So, you know, I would invite anyone who's got concerns about those applications in particular to be mindful of the context in which those panels were set up. I should add, however, that, apart from Stellar passage, which has obviously been subject to a separate judicial review process, you know, Taranaki and why he are working their way through the process, they are being led by retired senior court judges.

They've got very capable panels around them. And there will in fact be a draft decision released on the YE North expansion next week, I believe next Wednesday or uh, Tuesday or Wednesday. But as I said, I'm happy to answer any questions that you have about that process just to understand some of the challenges that we are already facing and setting up some panels.

Um, you know, my principal concerns are around those timeframes. 15 working days to set up a panel on a contentious application is not enough. And I can tell you that for those three specific projects, I would not have been able to set up the panel and do everything I needed to do and set the timeframe had I been constrained to 15 working days.

I would also tell you that those panels would not have been able to deliver a robust decision in 60 working days. Each of them was given longer than that, ranging I think between 75 and a hundred additional working days. And they will need all of that. Obviously, our specific concerns are detailed in our submission.

Uh, but just to reiterate our key message, we think the process is working well and it's delivering results that are fulfilling the purpose of the act in a way that is consistent with the procedural principles in Section 10 IE, timely, efficient, consistent, and cost effective. We have worked very hard to help achieve those results.

You've heard about the guidance that we've issued to panels. Sorry, Chair, sorry to interrupt you. We're gonna run Out of time. Oh, I'm sorry. I'm actually, I'm actually finished. Yeah, If you, if you just round up No, no, that's we open, that's open That, that, because we do have quite a few questions. No, that's fine. Questions. So we you sure You don't want to make any mark? No, no, I was really on the last point, so that's fine.

Okay. No, thank you. Thank you for that submission. Um, Rachel Brooking has the first question. Uh, thank, thank you. Thank you both. Look, I was very worried that panels would be set up and would be chaired for these complex projects by, um, planners and is are great. But you are, um, making the point well that in fact you have been appointed as the, uh, judge Jane as the, um, panel convener.

And both of you have worked very hard to have those very robust panels and it's a robustness. That is my question. You were in the room and you heard, uh, the parliamentary commissioner before and, um, and his, uh, lawyer talk about, uh, the risk of not enabling or changing the discretion that those panels have as to who they can, um, ask for evidence from.

So that's the, the clause, um, 50, the Commentary clause, is it? Yeah. Commentary clause. Who to invite for a comment? Yes. That, that clause, so removing that relying, um, very heavily on local government. Rebecca's comment was that the at risk for robust decision making that is defensible and then that will lead to more judicial reviews.

Mm. Can you, um, tell me if you agree with that or not? And then also maybe some examples of the types of, um, comment that the panels have been seeking. Don't need the details, just the ideas of the, um, the sorts of evidence that the panels have been able to obtain through the commentary that they would not have otherwise had the opportunity To obtain. Right.

And so I understand, um, the, the question is directed to, um, section 53, 53 and, uh, and at the moment, section 53, there are some people that you must invite and that won't change. You must invite the local authorities, the iwi authorities, the administrator agencies, doesn't change, must invite people who are also adjacent owners and occupies doesn't change.

But then the panel has under the current act a discretion to invite anybody else who they think appropriate to invite about that matter. We have put a lot of guidance in our guidance notice to how, how to approach the exercise of that because this is not your RMA business as usual, you know, effect, you know, invite people who are affected in some way.

It's not that it's something else. And the guidance note, seek to articulate what that might be. The, the law would change in as much as, uh, the pa the panel would, uh, need to invite, comment, um, or, or to solicit comment from the local authority as to the matters which the local authority are going to be commenting on themselves.

And then on that basis reach of you, is that sufficient in and of itself, or do we need to go further? Or if the local authority's not actually inviting that comment, then maybe we definitely need to go further. And the difficulty with that for a panel, um, and we are here, we are only talking about the first 10 working days now, uh, of the panel's existence, is that the panel is getting to grips, uh, with the application the local authorities are getting to grips with the application.

Uh, because not all applicants are revealing the applications to the local authorities before, uh, filing them with the EPA, the local authorities may not have, um, come to any view as to the matters which they would likely be making comment on, and therefore might not be in a position to respond.

Uh, when asked by the panel, what are the matters we're gonna be commenting on. I think the second part in terms of that, your question about, um, what questions have panels being asking of local authorities, I can't answer that in detail. I haven't been particularly tracking it. But I think the thing to bear in mind is this local authorities who are likely to respond, um, out of, uh, or consistently with their functions, they don't, they don't, uh, have a public interest function.

They may not have perfect knowledge and you may be expecting them to perhaps comment on business matters or matters of commerce or matters which might in impact, uh, property ownership rights or other private individual rights that I think is, is probably unrealistic and outside their statutory function.

So that's the thing to be, be, um, aware of. They may not, they may not be commenting because it's not their function to comment and they may not be adopting a public interest, um, role or advocacy. Thank you. Thank you. Look, we'll now move to Grant. Just A very quick question and we others have questions as well.

Um, you mentioned about the appointment of panelists and, you know, and trying to find good people to do this role. What the changes have been proposed here.
